title: Ezra 1
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Ezra
paragraphs: 1 In the first year of Cyrus, king of Persia, the LORD made His word through Jeremiah come true. The LORD moved the heart of Cyrus to send a public message across his kingdom. He also wrote it down like this:
2 "This is what Cyrus, king of Persia, says: 'The LORD, the God of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth. He has chosen me to build a house for Him at Jerusalem in Judah.
3 Any of you who belong to His people, may your God be with you. Go up to Jerusalem in Judah and build the house of the LORD, the God of Israel. He is the God who is in Jerusalem.

4 Every one of His people who is left, wherever he lives, should be helped by his neighbors. They should give him silver, gold, goods, and animals, along with a free gift for the house of God in Jerusalem.'"
5 So the family leaders of Judah and Benjamin got ready to go up. The priests and Levites went too. Every person whose heart God had moved prepared to rebuild the house of the LORD in Jerusalem.
6 All their neighbors helped them. They gave silver, gold, goods, animals, and other valuable things, on top of all their free gifts.
7 King Cyrus also brought out the objects from the house of the LORD. Nebuchadnezzar had taken them away from Jerusalem and put them in the temple of his own gods.
8 Cyrus, king of Persia, sent them out by the hand of Mithredath, the money officer. Mithredath counted them one by one for Sheshbazzar, the prince of Judah.
9 This was the full list: 30 gold dishes, 1,000 silver dishes, 29 silver tools,
10 30 gold bowls, 410 matching silver bowls, and 1,000 other objects.
11 In total, there were 5,400 gold and silver objects. Sheshbazzar brought all of these when the exiles went up from Babylon to Jerusalem.

title: Ezra 2
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Ezra
paragraphs: 1 These are the people of the province who came back from being captives in Babylon. King Nebuchadnezzar had taken them away. They returned to Jerusalem and Judah, each to his own town.
2 They came with Zerubbabel, Jeshua, Nehemiah, Seraiah, Reelaiah, Mordecai, Bilshan, Mispar, Bigvai, Rehum, and Baanah. This is the count of the men of Israel:
3 the family of Parosh, 2,172;
4 the family of Shephatiah, 372;
5 the family of Arah, 775;
6 the family of Pahath-moab (through Jeshua and Joab), 2,812;
7 the family of Elam, 1,254;
8 the family of Zattu, 945;
9 the family of Zaccai, 760;
10 the family of Bani, 642;
11 the family of Bebai, 623;
12 the family of Azgad, 1,222;
13 the family of Adonikam, 666;
14 the family of Bigvai, 2,056;
15 the family of Adin, 454;
16 the family of Ater (through Hezekiah), 98;
17 the family of Bezai, 323;
18 the family of Jorah, 112;
19 the family of Hashum, 223;
20 the family of Gibbar, 95;
21 the men of Bethlehem, 123;
22 the men of Netophah, 56;
23 the men of Anathoth, 128;
24 the family of Azmaveth, 42;
25 the men of Kiriath-jearim, Chephirah, and Beeroth, 743;
26 the men of Ramah and Geba, 621;
27 the men of Michmash, 122;
28 the men of Bethel and Ai, 223;
29 the family of Nebo, 52;
30 the family of Magbish, 156;
31 the family of the other Elam, 1,254;
32 the family of Harim, 320;
33 the men of Lod, Hadid, and Ono, 725;
34 the men of Jericho, 345;
35 and the family of Senaah, 3,630.
36 The priests: the family of Jedaiah (through the house of Jeshua), 973;
37 the family of Immer, 1,052;
38 the family of Pashhur, 1,247;
39 and the family of Harim, 1,017.
40 The Levites: the family of Jeshua and Kadmiel (through Hodaviah), 74.
41 The singers: the family of Asaph, 128.
42 The gatekeepers: the family of Shallum, the family of Ater, the family of Talmon, the family of Akkub, the family of Hatita, and the family of Shobai, 139 in all.
43 The temple servants: the family of Ziha, the family of Hasupha, the family of Tabbaoth,
44 the family of Keros, the family of Siaha, the family of Padon,
45 the family of Lebanah, the family of Hagabah, the family of Akkub,
46 the family of Hagab, the family of Shalmai, the family of Hanan,
47 the family of Giddel, the family of Gahar, the family of Reaiah,
48 the family of Rezin, the family of Nekoda, the family of Gazzam,
49 the family of Uzza, the family of Paseah, the family of Besai,
50 the family of Asnah, the family of Meunim, the family of Nephusim,
51 the family of Bakbuk, the family of Hakupha, the family of Harhur,
52 the family of Bazluth, the family of Mehida, the family of Harsha,
53 the family of Barkos, the family of Sisera, the family of Temah,
54 the family of Neziah, and the family of Hatipha.
55 The family of the servants of Solomon: the family of Sotai, the family of Sophereth, the family of Peruda,
56 the family of Jaala, the family of Darkon, the family of Giddel,
57 the family of Shephatiah, the family of Hattil, the family of Pochereth-hazzebaim, and the family of Ami.
58 The temple servants and the family of the servants of Solomon were 392 in all.
59 These people came up from Tel-melah, Tel-harsha, Cherub, Addan, and Immer. But they could not prove their family came from Israel:
60 the family of Delaiah, the family of Tobiah, and the family of Nekoda, 652 in all.
61 And from among the priests: the family of Hobaiah, the family of Hakkoz, and the family of Barzillai. (This man married a daughter of Barzillai the Gileadite and took her family name.)
62 These men looked for their family records. But they could not find them. So they were not allowed to serve as priests, as they were unclean.
63 The governor told them not to eat the most holy food until a priest could ask God through the Urim and Thummim.
64 The whole group together was 42,360 people.
65 That number did not count their 7,337 male and female servants, or their 200 male and female singers.
66 They had 736 horses and 245 mules,
67 435 camels, and 6,720 donkeys.
68 When they came to the house of the LORD in Jerusalem, some of the family heads gave gifts freely to rebuild the house of God on its original place.
69 They gave what they could to the treasury for this work: 61,000 darics of gold, 5,000 minas of silver, and 100 sets of priestly clothes.
70 So the priests, the Levites, the singers, the gatekeepers, and the temple servants, with some of the people, settled in their own towns. And the rest of the Israelites settled in their towns.

title: Ezra 3
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Ezra
paragraphs: 1 By the seventh month, the Israelites had settled in their towns. Then the people gathered together as one in Jerusalem.

2 Then Jeshua son of Jozadak and his fellow priests, along with Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and his helpers, began to build the altar of the God of Israel. They wanted to offer burnt offerings on it, as the Law of Moses the man of God says.
3 They set up the altar on its base. They offered burnt offerings to the LORD on it—both the morning and evening burnt offerings. They did this even though they feared the people of the land.
4 They also kept the Feast of Tabernacles, as it is written. They offered burnt offerings each day, by the number set for that day.

5 After that, they brought the regular burnt offerings. They also brought offerings for New Moon feasts and for all the set holy feasts of the LORD. They also brought all the gifts given freely to the LORD.
6 On the first day of the seventh month, the Israelites began to offer burnt offerings to the LORD. But the base of the LORD's temple had not yet been laid.

7 They gave money to the stone workers and wood workers. They gave food, drink, and oil to the people of Sidon and Tyre. In return, those people brought cedar logs from Lebanon to Joppa by sea. This was done with Cyrus king of Persia's permission.

8 In the second month of the second year after they reached the house of God in Jerusalem, the work began. Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, Jeshua son of Jozadak, and the rest of their helpers started it. This group included the priests, the Levites, and all who had come back to Jerusalem from being held in another land. They chose Levites twenty years old or older to watch over the building of the house of the LORD.

9 So Jeshua and his sons and brothers worked together. Kadmiel and his sons (descendants of Yehudah) joined them. The sons of Henadad and their sons and brothers also came—all Levites. They worked together to watch over those building the house of God.

10 The builders laid the base of the LORD's temple. Then the priests in their clothes stood with trumpets. The Levites (the sons of Asaph) stood with cymbals. They took their places to praise the LORD, as David king of Israel had set down.

11 And they sang back and forth with praise and thanksgiving to the LORD:  "For He is good; for His loving devotion to Israel endures forever."   Then all the people gave a great shout of praise to the LORD. They shouted because the base of the house of the LORD had been laid.
12 But many of the older priests, Levites, and family heads had seen the first temple. They cried out loud when they saw the base of this temple. Still, many others shouted with joy.
13 The people could not tell apart the shouts of joy from the sound of crying. The people made so much noise. And the sound was heard from far away.

title: Ezra 4
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Ezra
paragraphs: 1 The enemies of Judah and Benjamin heard that the people who had returned were building a temple for the LORD, the God of Israel.
2 So they came to Zerubbabel and the family leaders. They said, "Let us build with you. We also seek your God. We have offered sacrifices to Him since King Esar-haddon of Assyria brought us here."

3 But Zerubbabel, Jeshua, and the other family leaders of Israel answered, "You have no part with us in building a house for our God. We alone must build it for the LORD, the God of Israel. Cyrus king of Persia commanded us to do this."
4 Then the people of the land tried to make the people of Judah lose heart. They tried to make them afraid to build.
5 They also paid advisers to spoil their plans. This went on through the rule of Cyrus king of Persia, and even to the rule of Darius king of Persia.
6 At the start of the rule of Xerxes, the enemies wrote a complaint against the people of Judah and Jerusalem.
7 Later, in the days of Artaxerxes king of Persia, Bishlam, Mithredath, Tabeel, and their helpers wrote a letter to Artaxerxes. It was written in Aramaic and then translated.
8 Rehum the commander and Shimshai the scribe wrote this letter against Jerusalem to King Artaxerxes:
9 From Rehum the commander, Shimshai the scribe, and their helpers—the judges and officials over Tripolis, Persia, Erech, and Babylon, and the Elamites of Susa,
10 and the other peoples whom the great and noble Ashurbanipal forced to move and settle in the cities of Samaria and other places west of the Euphrates.
11 This is what the letter said: To King Artaxerxes, from your servants, the men west of the Euphrates:

12 Let the king know this. The Jews who came from you to us have returned to Jerusalem. They are rebuilding that evil city, the city that always fights against kings. They are fixing its walls and repairing its foundations.
13 Let the king also know this. If that city is rebuilt and its walls are fixed, the people will not pay taxes or fees. The king's money will be less.
14 We work for the palace. It is not right for us to let the king be shamed. So we are sending this message to inform the king.

15 Please search the record books of your fathers. In them you will find proof that this city has always fought against kings. It has caused trouble in kings' lands from old times. That is why this city was destroyed before.
16 We warn the king: if this city is rebuilt and its walls are fixed, you will have no power west of the Euphrates.
17 Then the king sent this reply: To Rehum the commander, Shimshai the scribe, and their helpers in Samaria and other places west of the Euphrates: Greetings.
18 The letter you sent has been translated and read in front of me.
19 I gave an order, and a search was done. We found that this city has turned against kings from old times. It has caused fighting and trouble.
20 Strong kings have ruled over Jerusalem. They ruled the whole region west of the Euphrates. Taxes and fees were paid to them.
21 So now, give an order for these men to stop. This city must not be rebuilt until I say so.
22 Be careful not to ignore this. Why let this danger grow and the king's interests suffer?
23 When the letter from King Artaxerxes was read to Rehum, Shimshai the scribe, and their helpers, they went at once to the Jews in Jerusalem. They used force to make them stop.
24 So the work on the house of God in Jerusalem stopped. It did not move forward until the second year of the rule of Darius king of Persia.

title: Ezra 5
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Ezra
paragraphs: 1 Later, two prophets spoke God's message to the Jews. They were Haggai and Zechariah son of Iddo. They spoke in Judah and Jerusalem in the name of the God of Israel, who watched over them.
2 Then Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Jeshua son of Jozadak stood up. They began to rebuild the house of God in Jerusalem. The prophets of God were with them and helped them.

3 At that time, Tattenai was governor of the land west of the Euphrates. He came with Shethar-bozenai and their fellow officers. They asked the Jews, "Who gave you permission to rebuild this temple and finish this building?"
4 They also asked, "What are the names of the men who are building this?"
5 But God was watching over the leaders of the Jews. So no one stopped them. The work went on until a report reached Darius. Then written orders about the matter came back.
6 This is the letter that Tattenai the governor of the land west of the Euphrates sent to King Darius. Shethar-bozenai and their fellow officers in that region sent it with him.
7 The report they sent him said: To King Darius: All peace.

8 Let the king know this. We went into the province of Judah, to the house of the great God. The people are rebuilding it with large stones. They are placing wood beams in the walls. The work is being done with great care and is going well.
9 So we questioned their leaders. We asked, "Who gave you permission to rebuild this temple and finish this building?"
10 We also asked for their names. We wanted to write down the names of their leaders for you.
11 This is the answer they gave us: "We are servants of the God of heaven and earth. We are rebuilding the temple that was built many years ago. A great king of Israel built and finished it.
12 But our fathers made the God of heaven angry. So He handed them over to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, the Chaldean. He destroyed this temple and took the people away to Babylon.
13 But in his first year, Cyrus king of Babylon gave an official order. He said the house of God should be rebuilt.

14 King Nebuchadnezzar had taken the gold and silver items from the temple in Jerusalem. He had carried them into the temple in Babylon. King Cyrus took these items out of that temple. He gave them to a man named Sheshbazzar, whom he made governor.
15 Cyrus told him, 'Take these items. Put them in the temple in Jerusalem. Let the house of God be rebuilt on its first place.'
16 So this Sheshbazzar came and started the base of the house of God in Jerusalem. From that time until now it has been being built, but it is not yet finished."

17 Now, if it pleases the king, please search the king's old records in Babylon. See if King Cyrus really gave an order to rebuild the house of God in Jerusalem. Then send us the king's decision about this matter.

title: Ezra 6
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Ezra
paragraphs: 1 So King Darius ordered his men to search the old records kept in the treasury of Babylon.
2 They found a scroll in the strong palace of Ecbatana, in the land of Media. This is what was written on it: A written note:

3 In the first year of King Cyrus, he gave an order about the house of God in Jerusalem: Let the house be built again as a place for offering sacrifices. Let its foundations be set firm. It must be sixty cubits high and sixty cubits wide.
4 It must have three layers of cut stones and one layer of wood beams. The costs must be paid from the king's treasury.

5 Also, the gold and silver items from the house of God, which Nebuchadnezzar took from the temple in Jerusalem and carried to Babylon, must be returned to the temple in Jerusalem. They must be placed in the house of God.
6 Then Darius said: To Tattenai, the governor west of the Euphrates, to Shethar-bozenai, and to your helpers and officials there: You must stay away from that place!
7 Leave this work on the house of God alone. Let the governor and the Jewish elders rebuild this house of God on its first site.

8 I now order what you must do for these Jewish elders who are rebuilding this house of God: The full cost must be paid to these men from the king's treasury. The money will come from the taxes of the lands west of the Euphrates, so the work is not stopped.

9 Whatever they need—young bulls, rams, and lambs for burnt offerings to the God of heaven, and wheat, salt, wine, and oil—must be given to them each day. The priests in Jerusalem will say what is needed.
10 Then they will be able to offer sacrifices with a sweet smell to the God of heaven. They will also pray for the life of the king and his sons.
11 I also order this: If any man changes this order, a beam must be torn from his house and set up. He must be nailed on it. His house must be made into a pile of broken stones.

12 May God, who has placed His Name there, throw down any king or people who tries to change this order or to destroy this house of God in Jerusalem. I, Darius, have given this order. Let it be done with care.
13 Then Tattenai, the governor west of the Euphrates, Shethar-bozenai, and their helpers carefully did what King Darius had ordered.

14 So the Jewish elders built and did well through the preaching of Haggai the prophet and Zechariah son of Iddo. They finished building, just as the God of Israel had commanded and just as Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes, kings of Persia, had ordered.
15 This temple was finished on the third day of the month of Adar, in the sixth year of the reign of King Darius.
16 Then the people of Israel—the priests, the Levites, and the rest of those who had come home—celebrated the dedication of the house of God with joy.

17 For the dedication of the house of God, they offered a hundred bulls, two hundred rams, and four hundred lambs. They also offered a sin offering for all Israel of twelve male goats, one for each tribe of Israel.
18 They put the priests in their groups and the Levites in their groups for the service of God in Jerusalem, as it is written in the Book of Moses.
19 On the fourteenth day of the first month, those who had come home kept the Passover.
20 All the priests and Levites had made themselves clean. They were clean for worship. The Levites killed the Passover lamb for all who had come home, for their priest brothers, and for themselves.
21 The Israelites who had returned from exile ate it. With them ate all who had turned away from the unclean ways of the people around them to seek the LORD, the God of Israel.

22 For seven days they kept the Feast of Unleavened Bread with joy. The LORD had made them glad. He had changed the heart of the king of Assyria toward them, so he helped them in the work on the house of the God of Israel.
